sources.name: Strunk & White, Elements of Style — Rule 13: Omit Needless Words
sources.note: Public domain. A sentence should contain no unnecessary words. Every modifier must earn its place.

sources.name: Strunk & White, Elements of Style — Rule 12: Use Definite, Specific, Concrete Language
sources.note: Prefer the specific to the general. Vague intensifiers replace specific claims with noise.

sources.name: Purdue OWL — Adjective or Adverb
sources.note: Adjective and adverb placement and common errors.

sources.name: Purdue OWL — How to Use Adjectives and Adverbs

_description: One adjective per noun by default. Two only if each adds distinct non-overlapping meaning. Adverbs modifying said or stated are blocked. Intensifiers with no precise meaning are deleted.

rules.rule: Use one adjective per noun by default.
rules.rationale: A single precise adjective carries more weight than two vague ones. Multiple adjectives often signal that none of them is doing its job.
rules.example_correct: A damaged bridge blocked the main road.
rules.example_incorrect: A badly damaged, severely compromised bridge blocked the main road.

rules.rule: Two adjectives are permitted only if each adds distinct, non-overlapping meaning.
rules.rationale: Two adjectives that describe different attributes of the noun — one structural, one evaluative — are justified. Two adjectives that say the same thing in different words are redundant.
rules.example_correct: A long, narrow corridor connected the two buildings.
rules.example_incorrect: A long, extended corridor connected the two buildings.
rules.note: long and narrow are distinct attributes. long and extended are the same attribute restated.

rules.rule: Adverbs modifying said or stated are blocked.
rules.rationale: Said and stated are neutral attribution verbs. Modifying them with adverbs — firmly stated, clearly said, openly declared — editorializes without adding factual content.
rules.example_correct: The minister said the border would remain closed.
rules.example_incorrect: The minister firmly stated that the border would remain closed.
rules.source: Strunk & White Rule 13 — omit needless words

rules.rule: Delete intensifiers that carry no precise meaning.
rules.rationale: Intensifiers like very, extremely, highly, incredibly, and particularly claim to strengthen the word they modify but do not. They add syllables without adding information. Delete them and use a stronger word if emphasis is needed.
rules.fix: Delete the intensifier. If emphasis is genuinely needed, replace the adjective with a stronger one.
rules.source: Strunk & White Rule 13 — very: use sparingly. Use words strong in themselves.

rules.rule: Place modifiers next to the word they modify.
rules.rationale: A modifier placed away from its target word creates ambiguity. The reader cannot tell which word is being modified.
rules.source: Strunk & White Rule 16 — keep related words together

rules.rule: Do not use not as an evasion. Use a direct positive or negative word.
rules.rationale: Negative constructions with not are weaker than direct positive or negative statements. The reader is told what something is not — not what it is.
rules.example_incorrect: He was not very often on time.
rules.source: Strunk & White Rule 11 — put statements in positive form

rules.rule: Do not use vague adjectives as filler. Name the specific quality.
rules.rationale: Adjectives like interesting, significant, important, and relevant announce that something matters without saying why. Replace with the specific claim.
rules.example_correct: Three checkpoints were unmanned during the night shift.
rules.example_incorrect: There were significant issues with checkpoint coverage during the night shift.
rules.fix: Name what is interesting, significant, or important. Do not label it.
rules.source: Strunk & White Rule 12 — use definite, specific, concrete language
